name: ReactJs
scope: An individual with INTERMEDIATE proficiency in React has 3-6 years of experience and a solid grasp of React's core concepts. They can create functional and class components, manage state and props, and understand component lifecycle methods. Familiar with state management libraries like Redux or MobX, they proficiently use hooks—including custom hooks—for better code reusability. They implement routing with React Router, handle forms and validation using libraries like Formik or Yup, and integrate APIs using fetch or Axios. Skilled in styling techniques, they utilize CSS-in-JS libraries and ensure responsive design. They optimize performance through memoization and lazy loading, and write unit tests using Jest and React Testing Library. Familiar with build tools like Webpack and Babel, they can deploy applications to platforms like Netlify or AWS Amplify. They collaborate effectively using Git, participate in code reviews, and are accustomed to Agile methodologies.
long_scope:
Scope definition for INTERMEDIATE-level proficiency in ReactJs (typical 3-5 years focused experience)

1. Advanced hooks and functional component patterns
• Use all standard hooks (useState, useEffect, useContext, useReducer, useRef, useMemo, useCallback, useLayoutEffect) with a clear understanding of their purpose and trade-offs
• Build custom hooks that encapsulate reusable stateful logic such as form handling, debouncing, local storage sync, and API fetching
• Implement useReducer for complex state transitions that involve multiple related values or action-based updates
• Understand hook rules (call order, no conditional hooks) and diagnose stale closure issues in useEffect and event handlers
• Compose multiple custom hooks together to build feature-level abstractions that simplify component code

2. State management with Context API and external libraries
• Design Context providers for shared application state such as authentication, theming, and user preferences
• Implement Redux or Zustand for global state management in medium-to-large applications with multiple feature domains
• Configure Redux Toolkit with createSlice, createAsyncThunk, and RTK Query for standardized state and data fetching patterns
• Split context into multiple providers to avoid unnecessary re-renders when only part of the global state changes
• Evaluate state management approaches and select the appropriate tool based on application size and team familiarity

3. Routing and navigation with React Router
• Configure React Router v6 with nested routes, layout routes, index routes, and route-based code splitting
• Implement programmatic navigation using useNavigate, route parameters with useParams, and query strings with useSearchParams
• Build protected route patterns that redirect unauthenticated users and preserve intended destinations after login
• Handle route transitions with loading indicators and error boundaries for route-level data fetching
• Implement breadcrumb navigation and active link styling using React Router utilities

4. Performance optimization techniques
• Apply React.memo, useMemo, and useCallback strategically based on profiling data rather than premature optimization
• Implement code splitting with React.lazy and Suspense to reduce initial bundle size and improve load times
• Use the React DevTools Profiler to identify components that re-render unnecessarily and apply targeted fixes
• Implement list virtualization using libraries like react-window for efficiently rendering large datasets
• Optimize images and assets within React applications using lazy loading, responsive images, and modern formats

5. Testing with Jest and React Testing Library
• Write unit tests that verify component behavior from the users perspective using queries like getByRole and getByText
• Test user interactions including clicks, form input, keyboard navigation, and async operations with waitFor and findBy
• Mock API calls, context providers, and external dependencies to isolate components during testing
• Achieve meaningful test coverage for critical user flows and business logic without over-testing implementation details
• Implement integration tests that verify multiple components working together within a feature workflow

6. API integration and data management
• Implement robust data fetching patterns with proper loading, error, and empty state handling across the application
• Use React Query, SWR, or RTK Query for server state management with automatic caching, refetching, and background updates
• Handle authentication tokens in API requests using interceptors or middleware with token refresh capabilities
• Implement optimistic updates for common user actions to provide instant UI feedback before server confirmation
• Manage paginated data, infinite scrolling, and search-as-you-type patterns with proper debouncing and cache management

7. TypeScript integration with React
• Define typed component props using interfaces and type aliases, including optional props, union types, and generic props
• Type hooks including useState with generic types, useRef for DOM elements, and custom hooks with proper return type signatures
• Use discriminated unions for component prop variants that enable different behaviors based on prop combinations
• Type event handlers, form events, and callback functions with React-specific event types
• Configure and maintain tsconfig.json settings appropriate for React projects with strict type checking enabled

8. Next.js and server-side rendering concepts
• Understand the differences between client-side rendering, server-side rendering, static site generation, and incremental static regeneration
• Build pages in Next.js using the App Router or Pages Router with appropriate data fetching methods (getServerSideProps, getStaticProps, or server components)
• Implement API routes in Next.js for backend functionality including form handlers, authentication endpoints, and data proxies
• Configure dynamic routes, catch-all routes, and middleware in Next.js for flexible page routing
• Understand hydration, the relationship between server-rendered HTML and client-side React, and common hydration mismatch issues

9. Styling approaches and design systems
• Implement CSS-in-JS solutions (styled-components, Emotion) or utility-first CSS (Tailwind) within component architectures
• Build and consume a shared component library with consistent theming, spacing, typography, and color tokens
• Implement responsive design patterns using CSS media queries, container queries, or responsive utility classes within React
• Create dark mode and theme switching functionality using CSS custom properties or theme context providers
• Maintain style consistency across a team by establishing and enforcing styling conventions and shared design tokens

10. CI/CD and development workflows
• Configure ESLint and Prettier for React projects with appropriate rules for hooks, accessibility, and code formatting
• Set up pre-commit hooks using Husky and lint-staged to enforce code quality before commits
• Understand CI/CD pipeline stages including linting, testing, building, and deploying React applications
• Configure environment variables for different deployment environments (development, staging, production)
• Use feature flags or environment-based configuration to manage feature rollouts and A/B testing

11. Error handling and application resilience
• Implement Error Boundaries to catch rendering errors and display fallback UI without crashing the entire application
• Design error handling strategies that provide meaningful feedback to users while logging details for developers
• Handle network failures gracefully with retry logic, offline detection, and cached fallback content
• Implement global error tracking using tools like Sentry to capture and monitor production errors
• Build toast notification and alert systems that communicate success, warning, and error states consistently

12. Mentorship and team contribution
• Mentor junior developers on React fundamentals, component design patterns, and debugging techniques
• Contribute to architectural decisions by evaluating trade-offs between different React patterns and library choices
• Conduct thorough code reviews focused on React best practices, performance implications, and maintainability
• Document shared patterns, component APIs, and architectural decisions for team reference
• Lead feature implementation from technical design through testing and deployment with minimal senior oversight

A practitioner who fulfills all parts of this scope can independently build complex single-page applications, make sound architectural decisions for React projects, optimize performance, integrate TypeScript and testing, and contribute to team growth through mentorship and technical leadership.
